Section 14.18
USING DEPT TO COUNT HYDROGENS
©2020 McGraw-Hill Education. All rights reserved. Authorized only for instructor use in the classroom. No reproduction or further distribution permitted without the prior written consent of McGraw-Hill Education .

The Pulsed-FT NMR Experiment
The pulsed - FT NMR experiment has four stages:
Application of a magnetic field B 0 ; equilibration of nuclear spins
Application of a rf pulse to excite all nuclei to the higher - energy spin state
Acquisition of emitted rf light data over time as nuclei relax
Transformation of the time data to the frequency domain

DEPT
The pulsed-FT NMR experiment has four stages:
Application of a magnetic field B 0 ; equilibration of nuclear spins
Application of a rf pulse to excite all nuclei to the higher-energy spin state
Acquisition of emitted rf light data over time as nuclei relax
Transformation of the time data to the frequency domain
Additional pulses of rf radiation during the experiment can provide additional information.
In a DEPT 13 C experiment, 1 H ’s are excited differently depending on the number of geminal 1 H (0, 1, or 2).

DEPT Spectra: Determining CH n
In a normal 13 C spectrum, all peaks have positive phase

DEPT Spectra: Determining CH n -1
One DEPT pulse sequence inverts the phase of CH 2 carbons, revealing methylenes

DEPT Spectra: Determining CH n -2
Other pulse sequences reveal other counts of hydrogens, allowing assignment of CH n for all 13 C signals

Section 14.19
2D NMR: COSY AND HETCOR

Two-dimensional NMR
The spectra we’ve looked at so far are one-dimensional —they have a single frequency axis
Two-dimensional NMR spectra have two frequency axes and use contour lines to show intensity
2D NMR spectra reveal connectivity by showing “what’s coupling to what”
We will study two types:
Correlation spectroscopy (COSY): 1 H – 1 H coupling
Heteronuclear correlation spectroscopy (HETCOR): 1 H – 13 C coupling

COSY
The same 1 H NMR spectrum is plotted twice; off - diagonal peaks point to coupled protons

COSY-1
The same 1 H NMR spectrum is plotted twice; off-diagonal peaks point to coupled protons

HETCOR
1 H spectrum on one axis; 13 C on the other
Peaks show which hydrogens are attached to which carbons

Section 14.20
INTRODUCTION TO INFRARED SPECTROSCOPY

Molecular Vibrations
Atoms in molecules vibrate like balls attached to springs
Infrared light can be used to excite these vibrations; the precise frequency depends on the strength of the vibrating bond(s)
Large molecules have many different ways to vibrate. Consider CH 2 :

Molecular Vibrations-1
Atoms in molecules vibrate like balls attached to springs
Infrared light can be used to excite these vibrations; the precise frequency depends on the strength of the vibrating bond(s)
Large molecules have many different ways to vibrate. Consider CH 2 :

Section 14.21
INFRARED SPECTRA
Regions in IR Spectra
Example: Hexane
Example: 1-Hexene

Example: Benzene
Note the trend in C–H stretching frequencies !

Section 14.22
CHARACTERISTIC ABSORPTION FREQUENCIES

Alcohols
Alcohols universally have a broad peak around 3200–3400 cm − 1 for the O–H stretch
Nitriles

Carbonyl Compounds
The carbonyl stretch is very characteristic: a strong, sharp peak around 1700 cm − 1

Amines
Amine N – H stretches are weak and show up around 3300–3500 cm − 1
Primary amines (RNH 2 ) show two peaks in this region; secondary amines (HNR 2 ) show only one
Other Characteristic Peaks
Other Characteristic Peaks-1

Trends in 𝛌 𝐦𝐚𝐱 -1

Section 14.24
MASS SPECTROMETRY

Mass Spectrometry
Mass spectrometry is not spectroscopy at all, but it is a valuable technique for structural characterization
A sample is bombarded with an electron beam, causing the ejection of electrons and the formation of radical cations
The cation can be “steered” and its mass determined by an electromagnetic field
The resulting molecular ion can fragment into other molecules
Anatomy of a Mass Spectrometer

Mass Spectra
A mass spectrum shows the abundance of ions of various mass-to-charge ( m/z ) ratios
The base peak and the molecular ion peak may not coincide !
The peak of maximum height is called the base peak
The base peak here is also the molecular ion peak at the molecular weight of benzene (78)
Why are there small peaks above 78 ?!

Isotope Effects
A small percentage of benzene molecules contain 13 C or 2 H and have a mass of 79
Bromine ( 79 Br ∶ 81 Br = 1∶1) and chlorine ( 35 Cl ∶ 37 Cl = 3∶1) are famous for these effects
Chlorobenzene

Fragmentation
Fragments can provide structural insights
Single atoms may break off, as in the spectrum of chlorobenzene
Peaks in mass spectra of alkanes are often separated by 14 units (RCH 2 R)

Fragmentation-1
Fragmentation often occurs to give stable carbocations
For example, benzylic bonds cleave to give stable benzylic cations

Section 14.25
MOLECULAR FORMULA AS A CLUE TO STRUCTURE

The Nitrogen Rule
A molecule with an odd number of nitrogens has an odd molecular weight; a molecule with an even number of nitrogens or only C, H, and O has an even molecular weight

High-resolution MS
Compounds of similar molecular weight but different formulas can be distinguished by high - resolution mass spectrometry

High-resolution MS-1
Compounds of similar molecular weight but different formulas can be distinguished by high - resolution mass spectrometry
100 g/ mol
100.1253 g/ mol
100 g/ mol
100.0524 g/ mol
Molecular ions for these two compounds can be distinguished in a high - resolution mass spectrum.

Index of Hydrogen Deficiency
Multiple bonds and rings replace H 2 in hydrocarbons. Each “missing” H 2 corresponds to one multiple bond or one ring
The index of hydrogen deficiency is equal to the number of multiple bonds or rings in the hydrocarbon

Index of Hydrogen Deficiency-1
Multiple bonds and rings replace H 2 in hydrocarbons. Each “missing” H 2 corresponds to one multiple bond or one ring
The index of hydrogen deficiency is equal to the number of multiple bonds or rings in the hydrocarbon
For compounds with heteroatoms, inside the parentheses…
Add 1 for each halogen
Subtract 1 for each nitrogen
